title: Scoring Context Candidates
concept: Assigning a relevance score to each candidate piece of context before deciding what makes the cut.

exercises.prompt: Since this is the start of a new unit, let's warm up: When an AI app has too much text to fit into the model's context window, what's the smartest move?

options.text: Pick only the pieces that matter most for the question
Send everything and hope the model figures it out
Send nothing and let the model guess

exercises.explain: Context windows are limited, so smart apps must choose the most useful pieces of information rather than dumping everything in or leaving it empty.

cards.title: Too Many Documents, Too Little Room
cards.body: Imagine you asked an AI assistant a question and it had 200 documents it could use, but only room for 5. How does it pick which 5 win?

cards.title: What Is a Relevance Score?
cards.body: A relevance score is a number given to each piece of context (like a chunk of text) that estimates how useful it is for answering the current question. Higher score means more likely to help; lower score means it's probably not needed.

cards.title: Like Judging a Talent Show
cards.body: Imagine every candidate piece of context is a performer auditioning for a show. Each one gets a score from the judges based on how well it fits what the audience (your question) wants. Only the highest scorers make it onto the stage.

cards.title: Searching for a Recipe
cards.body: If you ask 'How do I make banana bread?', a chunk about 'banana bread ingredients' might score 0.9, a chunk about 'banana nutrition facts' might score 0.4, and a chunk about 'car repair tips' might score 0.05. The scores help the system know what's actually worth keeping.

cards.title: Myth: Longer Text Means More Relevant
cards.body: A long document isn't automatically more relevant than a short one. Scoring looks at how closely the content matches the meaning of the question, not how many words it has.

cards.title: Scoring vs. Guessing
cards.body: Without scoring, an app just grabs random or recent text. With scoring, every candidate gets ranked by usefulness before anything is chosen.

left.text: Random chunks get included, some totally unrelated to the question

right.text: Each chunk gets a relevance number, so the most useful ones rise to the top

exercises.prompt: What does a relevance score tell an AI system about a piece of context?

options.text: How likely that piece of context is to help answer the current question
How recently the text was written
How many words are in all caps

exercises.explain: A relevance score measures usefulness for the specific question being asked, not length, recency, or formatting.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Every candidate piece of context must be scored before deciding what to keep or cut.
exercises.explain: Scoring happens first for every candidate so the system has a fair basis to compare them and decide which ones make the final cut.

exercises.sentence: A relevance score estimates how useful a piece of context is for answering the current question.
exercises.explain: The term 'relevance score' specifically names the number used to judge usefulness for the question, unlike word count or file size which don't measure meaning.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scoring explanations sounds like it was generated by an AI trying to sound impressive but is actually vague and unhelpful?

options.text: This chunk scores 0.87 because it directly mentions the ingredients and steps asked about in the question.
This chunk represents a paradigm of contextual synergy that optimally leverages informational resonance for the query.
This chunk scores low because it talks about a different topic than what the question is asking.

exercises.explain: The second option uses fancy-sounding but empty phrases like 'contextual synergy' and 'informational resonance' without explaining anything concrete about why the score was given—classic AI filler.

exercises.prompt: You're building an AI app that answers customer questions using 500 saved support articles. Before picking which articles to show the model, what should happen to each article first?

options.text: Each article gets a relevance score based on how well it matches the customer's question
All 500 articles get sent to the model at once
Articles are chosen randomly to save time
Only the newest articles are used, regardless of topic

exercises.explain: Before deciding what makes the cut, every candidate needs a relevance score so the system can compare them fairly and pick the ones most likely to help—this is the foundation of relevance-based prioritization you'll build on in the rest of this unit.
